text: The surgeon uses tooth forceps to grasp the fundus of the gallbladder and retract it, exposing the area for dissection. Adhesions between the duodenum and gallbladder are separated, revealing the duodenum moving downward as the gallbladder is pushed upward into view.

The surgeon uses a Hartman's pouch grasper to manipulate the lower portion of the gallbladder while holding the fundus with another forceps. A Maryland Dissector is used to remove adhesions and peritoneum from both sides of the gallbladder. The roundish lymph node is visible, and the surgeon approaches the cystic duct, with the common bile duct clearly seen below.
The surgeon performs a posterior calyx angle dissection between the gallbladder and the cystic duct, creating a visible window between these structures.

The surgeon peels away fibro fatty tissue and fascia to delineate the gallbladder and its connections with the common hepatic duct and common bile duct. The cystic artery, which supplies the gallbladder, is visible dividing into two parts. A lymph node, identified as the lymph node of Lund, is torn during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, noting its position as an indicator of the main artery supplying the gallbladder. The artery is visible, pushing blood towards the gallbladder, and the surgeon prepares to block this vessel. The cystic duct is also clearly visible, connecting the gallbladder to the common bile duct, with clots being removed to enhance visibility. The critical view of safety is achieved, showing both the cystic artery and duct from posterior and anterior perspectives.

The surgeon exposes the gallbladder fossa, revealing the bed of the gallbladder lying on the liver. The lower one-third portion is cleared, providing a clear view of the common bile duct below. The cystic duct and cystic artery are identified, and preparations are made to clip and cut these structures, ensuring clarity and precision in the dissection.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator to enclose the posterior branch of the cystic artery, ensuring the tip of the clip is visible. The clip is pressed firmly to prevent slipping. The area is further delineated for clear visibility, and a second clip is loaded and applied to both branches of the cystic artery.

The surgeon applies two clips on the cystic duct, slightly above its junction with the common bile duct, and then applies a second clip on the cystic artery. A distal clip is placed on the cystic artery, and the surgeon prepares to cut between the distal and proximal clips.

The surgeon applies a clip to prevent spillover of bile and small stones from the gallbladder. The main branch of the cystic artery, specifically the right-sided or anterior branch, is cut. The posterior branch is delineated by pushing fibro-fatty tissue away using scissors, ensuring a secure cut.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery hook to carefully separate the peritoneal connection of the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring not to apply excessive pressure to avoid injuring the gallbladder. The pulsation in the cystic artery is visible, and the cystic duct is located below. The hook is maneuvered under the fibrofacial tissue and peritoneum to prevent bile leakage or stone slippage.
